content:
# Genesis Chapter 13 — Disagreement Report
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Genesis 13 (Abram and Lot Separate, 18 verses)
**Translators in Council:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Date of Arbitration:** 2026-03-04

---

## Summary

Genesis 13 presented **nine disagreements** across the three translators, fewer than the eleven recorded for Genesis 12. The relative reduction reflects the nature of the chapter: its narrative is comparatively clear, its vocabulary concrete, and its theological architecture transparent enough that literal, formal, and dynamic approaches arrived at substantially similar readings on most verses. The disagreements that did arise were focused and meaningful, concentrating in four areas: (1) the rendering of the Hebrew idiom כָּבֵד ("heavy") for wealth at verse 2, (2) the syntactic handling of the conditional pair at verse 9, (3) the word order of the Sodom characterization at verse 13, and (4) the rendering of הִתְהַלֵּךְ ("walk about / walk through") at verse 17. Several additional minor disagreements in register and phrasing are documented below.

Agreement was very high on the theological core of the chapter — the double simile of verse 10 (Eden and Egypt), the dust promise of verse 16, the directional survey of verse 14, and the altar at verse 18. All three translators converged on these without significant variation. The LXX's substitution of "sand" for "dust" at verse 16 and "paradise" for "garden" at verse 10 were rejected unanimously by all three translators.

The chapter's structural irony — Lot choosing by sight, Abram receiving by promise — was identified and articulated by all three translators with remarkable unanimity of interpretation, though PNEUMA developed the most extended literary analysis, EMET the most detailed textual apparatus, and LOGOS the most precise structural mapping. The council affirms all three approaches as complementary.

---

## Disagreement Index

| # | Verse(s) | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|----------|-------|------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | 1 | Opening particle — "And" / "So" | "And Abram went up" | "And Abram went up" | "So Abram came up" | **"So Abram went up"** |
| 2 | 2 | כָּבֵד מְאֹד — "very heavy" vs. "very rich" | "very heavy in livestock" | "very rich in livestock" | "very wealthy" | **"very rich in livestock"** |
| 3 | 5 | צֹאן וּבָקָר וְאֹהָלִים — "flocks and cattle and tents" | "flocks and cattle and tents" | "flocks and herds and tents" | "flocks and herds and tents" | **"flocks and herds and tents"** |
| 4 | 8 | אַחִים — "brothers" vs. "kinsmen" vs. "family" | "we are men brothers" | "we are kinsmen" | "we are family" | **"we are kinsmen"** |
| 5 | 9 | Conditional structure — "left/right" formulation | "if to the left... if to the right" | "if you take the left hand... if you take the right hand" | "if you go left... if you go right" | **"If you go to the left... if you go to the right"** |
| 6 | 13 | Placement of מְאֹד ("exceedingly") | "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly" | "exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD" | "deeply wicked — flagrant sinners against the LORD" | **"wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly"** |
| 7 | 14 | Opening verb — "The LORD said" vs. "And the LORD said" | "And the LORD said" | "The LORD said" | "the LORD said to Abram" | **"The LORD said to Abram"** |
| 8 | 17 | הִתְהַלֵּךְ — "walk about" vs. "walk through" vs. "walk" | "walk about in the land through its length and through its breadth" | "walk through the length and the breadth of the land" | "Walk the length and breadth of the land" | **"walk through the land, through its length and through its breadth"** |
| 9 | 18 | Opening conjunction — "And" / "So" | "And Abram pitched his tent and came and dwelt" | "So Abram moved his tent and came and settled" | "So Abram moved his tents and came to live" | **"So Abram moved his tent and came and settled"** |

---

## Disagreement Log — Full Detail

---

### DISAGREEMENT 1 — Verse 1: Opening Particle — "And" / "So"

**Hebrew:** וַיַּעַל אַבְרָם מִמִּצְרַיִם (*vayyaal Avram mimMitsrayim*, "and Abram went up from Egypt")
**Nature:** Register and discourse — the waw-consecutive rendering at a chapter opening
**Severity:** Low — affects the chapter's relationship to what precedes it (Genesis 12:20)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "And Abram went up from Egypt" | Waw-consecutive rendered literally; maintains narrative continuity as direct sequel to 12:20 |
| LOGOS | "And Abram went up from Egypt" | Agrees with EMET; the chapter opens mid-story, directly following Pharaoh's dismissal |
| PNEUMA | "So Abram came up out of Egypt" | "So" renders the consequential waw; "came up out of" expands the directional verb expressively; "out of Egypt" rather than "from Egypt" |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "So Abram went up from Egypt" — a blend that adopts PNEUMA's consequential "So" while retaining LOGOS/EMET's more precise "went up" over PNEUMA's "came up out of."

Genesis 13:1 is the direct continuation of 12:20 (Pharaoh's dismissal of Abram). The waw-consecutive here carries a consequential force: *as a result of* the dismissal, Abram went up. "So" better captures this consequence than "And," which sounds too flat a continuation. EMET and LOGOS both have "And," which is defensible but misses the narrative force of the connective. PNEUMA's "So" is adopted.

"Went up" is preferred over PNEUMA's "came up out of" for concision and for the preservation of the simple directional verb עָלָה (alah). "Out of" imports an element of exodus-from-confinement that, while theologically resonant, is an addition to the Hebrew. The Vaticanus reads Ἀνέβη δὲ Αβραμ — "And Abram went up" — with δέ as the connective particle (slightly adversative/transitional); "So" adequately renders this transition.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 2 — Verse 2: כָּבֵד מְאֹד — "Very Heavy" vs. "Very Rich"

**Hebrew:** וְאַבְרָם כָּבֵד מְאֹד בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב (*ve'Avram kaved me'od bamiqneh bakesef uvazahav*)
**Nature:** Lexical — the Hebrew idiom כָּבֵד for wealth vs. its literal translation
**Severity:** Moderate — EMET's "heavy" preserves a wordplay with 12:10; "rich" is standard but loses it

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "And Abram was very heavy in livestock, in silver, and in gold" | Literal: כָּבֵד means "heavy"; preserves the wordplay with כָּבֵד הָרָעָב ("the famine was heavy," 12:10); the heavy famine → heavy wealth irony is the literary point |
| LOGOS | "Now Abram was very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold" | Renders the idiom into standard English; "rich" is the semantic equivalent of כָּבֵד in a wealth context; notes the LXX also normalizes to πλούσιος ("wealthy") |
| PNEUMA | "Abram was very wealthy: rich in livestock, silver, and gold" | Agrees with the semantic range; compresses the tripartite catalogue slightly |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "Now Abram was very rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold" — LOGOS is followed.

EMET makes a compelling case for "heavy." The כָּבֵד wordplay between 12:10 ("the famine was *heavy*") and 13:2 ("Abram was *heavy* with wealth") is a genuine literary irony: the weight of scarcity transforms into the weight of abundance. This is exactly the kind of lexical patterning that EMET's literal mandate is designed to preserve, and the council takes it seriously.

However, "Abram was very heavy in livestock" in English cannot be read as a statement of wealth without prior knowledge of the Hebrew idiom. Unlike the כָּבֵד of 12:10, which "heavy famine" renders naturally (a famine *is* described as heavy in English idiom — a heavy blow, a heavy burden), a person being "heavy" *in livestock* is opaque. The reader encounters an oddity without a payoff they can access. The council therefore follows LOGOS and the LXX (πλούσιος σφόδρα, "very wealthy") in rendering "very rich," while preserving EMET's wordplay analysis in Council Note 1 of the consensus translation.

PNEUMA's further compression of the tripartite catalogue ("livestock, silver, and gold" without the repeated preposition בַּ) is rejected. The three-fold בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב (in livestock / in silver / and in gold) is a deliberate enumerative structure; preserving the repeated preposition "in" ("in livestock, in silver, and in gold") retains the texture of the catalogue.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 3 — Verse 5: צֹאן וּבָקָר וְאֹהָלִים — "Flocks and Cattle" vs. "Flocks and Herds"

**Hebrew:** צֹאן וּבָקָר וְאֹהָלִים (*tson uvakar ve'ohalim*)
**Nature:** Lexical — whether to render בָּקָר as "cattle" or "herds"
**Severity:** Low — the meaning is essentially equivalent; the question is English naturalness

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "flocks and cattle and tents" | בָּקָר (baqar) means "cattle/bovines" specifically; "cattle" is the precise English equivalent |
| LOGOS | "flocks and herds and tents" | "Herds" is the natural English pair for "flocks" in pastoral contexts; "cattle" is more specific than the context requires; "flocks and herds" is the established English Bible idiom |
| PNEUMA | "flocks and herds and tents" | Agrees with LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "flocks and herds and tents" — LOGOS and PNEUMA carry the majority.

Hebrew **בָּקָר** (baqar) does specifically mean cattle (bovines), and EMET is technically correct that "cattle" is more precise than "herds." However, **צֹאן** (tson, "flocks") and **בָּקָר** (baqar, "cattle/herds") form a standard Hebrew pairing for the two primary categories of pastoral livestock — small animals (sheep and goats) and large animals (cattle, bovines). In English, the established translation idiom for this pair is "flocks and herds," which is used throughout the biblical pastoral narratives (Genesis 13, 24, 26, 32, etc.) and is already the established SOPHIA rendering from Genesis 12. "Flocks and herds" is both accurate and idiomatic; "flocks and cattle" introduces a slight asymmetry (one category designation + one specific animal type). The council follows LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 4 — Verse 8: אַחִים — "Brothers" / "Kinsmen" / "Family"

**Hebrew:** כִּי-אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ (*ki-anashim akhim anakhnu*, "for we are men brothers")
**Nature:** Lexical — the range of אָח (akh) in the phrase אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים
**Severity:** Moderate — affects whether the appeal is to kinship precision, to family warmth, or to communal brotherhood

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "for we are men brothers" | Literal rendering of the apposition אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים; preserves "men" as a dignity-marker; "brothers" preserves the Hebrew word over its more precise English gloss |
| LOGOS | "for we are kinsmen" | אָח in the broad sense = kinsmen; Abram and Lot are uncle and nephew, not biological brothers; "kinsmen" is more accurate to the actual relationship; the LXX (ἄνθρωποι ἀδελφοί) supports preserving "brothers" but LOGOS chooses to translate for the actual relationship |
| PNEUMA | "for we are family" | Maximum accessibility; "family" conveys the relational claim without specifying the kinship structure; natural in contemporary English |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "for we are kinsmen" — LOGOS is followed.

EMET's "for we are men brothers" is the most literal rendering and preserves the unusual apposition of אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים ("men, brothers"). The word אֲנָשִׁים ("men") may carry a connotation of standing or dignity — the phrase could be rendered "for we are men of standing who are family to each other." This nuance is lost in any single-word rendering. However, "we are men brothers" is awkward in English to the point of obscuring the appeal rather than conveying it.

"We are family" (PNEUMA) is warm and accessible but loses the specificity of the Hebrew kinship claim entirely. אָח is not merely "family" but a defined relational category — and Abram is invoking it precisely to appeal to Lot's sense of family obligation. The vagueness of "family" is a loss, not an equivalent.

"We are kinsmen" (LOGOS) preserves the kinship specificity, is accurate to the actual relationship (uncle and nephew, generously called brothers), and is natural English. The LXX reads ἄνθρωποι ἀδελφοί ἐσμεν ἡμεῖς ("we are men who are brothers"), supporting the "brothers" dimension; the council notes this but follows LOGOS's interpretive choice to render the relationship as it actually is. The council preserves EMET's analysis of the dignity-connotation of אֲנָשִׁים in Council Note 4.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 5 — Verse 9: The Left/Right Conditional Structure

**Hebrew:** אִם-הַשְּׂמֹאל וְאֵימִנָה וְאִם-הַיָּמִין וְאַשְׂמְאִילָה (*im-hashemo'l ve'eyminah ve'im-hayamin ve'ashme'ilah*)
**Nature:** Syntactic — how to render the conditional parallel in natural English
**Severity:** Low-moderate — the syntactic structure encodes Abram's deference; how it is rendered affects the clarity of that gesture

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "if to the left then I will go right and if to the right then I will go left" | Most literal; preserves the terse parallel structure of the Hebrew; the verbs at the end of each clause capture the Hebrew word order |
| LOGOS | "If you take the left hand, then I will go to the right, or if you take the right hand, then I will go to the left" | Expands the elliptical Hebrew with explicit subject ("you") and object ("the hand"); the addition is necessary for English comprehension |
| PNEUMA | "if you go left, I will go right; if you go right, I will go left" | Most compressed; semicolon creates a sharper contrast; the terseness better captures the Hebrew's epigrammatic quality |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "If you go to the left, then I will go to the right; or if you go to the right, then I will go to the left" — a synthesis.

EMET's "if to the left then I will go right" follows the Hebrew ellipsis (the "to the left" has no explicit subject or verb in Hebrew; it is: "if [it is] the left, then I will go right"). In English this reads as slightly fragmented. LOGOS's expansion "if you take the left hand" is clear and accurate but the idiom "take the left hand" is slightly formal and archaic. PNEUMA's "if you go left... if you go right" is the most natural contemporary English but drops "then" and compresses out the parallel structure.

The council adopts a synthesis: "If you go to the left, then I will go to the right; or if you go to the right, then I will go to the left." This:
- Supplies the explicit subject "you" from Lot's perspective (following LOGOS's logic)
- Preserves the parallel conditional structure with "then" (following EMET's structure)
- Uses "go to the left / go to the right" for English naturalness (following PNEUMA's directional phrasing)
- Connects the two conditionals with "or" (more precise than a semicolon, which implies equality of alternatives without linking them)

---

### DISAGREEMENT 6 — Verse 13: Placement of מְאֹד ("Exceedingly") in the Sodom Characterization

**Hebrew:** וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד (*ve'anshey Sedom ra'im vehatta'im laYHWH me'od*)
**Nature:** Syntactic — the placement of the intensifier מְאֹד (me'od, "exceedingly") in the English rendering
**Severity:** Moderate — the word order encodes which terms the intensifier modifies

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly" | Preserves Hebrew word order: מְאֹד falls at the end, after the entire compound; it modifies the whole statement, not only the final adjective |
| LOGOS | "exceedingly wicked and sinful against the LORD" | Moves מְאֹד to the front for English emphasis; "exceedingly wicked" is more natural English than "wicked... exceedingly" |
| PNEUMA | "deeply wicked — flagrant sinners against the LORD" | Abandons direct rendering of מְאֹד entirely; "deeply" and "flagrant" are interpretive equivalents; loses the single intensifier in favor of two separate strong adjectives |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "wicked and sinful against the LORD exceedingly" — EMET's word order is adopted.

This is one of the few disagreements in Genesis 13 where the council follows EMET over LOGOS on a matter of word order, and the reason is specifically the Hebrew syntax.

The Hebrew places מְאֹד at the end of the entire compound: **רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד** — the two adjectives (wicked + sinful) with the directional phrase "against the LORD" come first, and then מְאֹד falls like a weight at the end. In this position, מְאֹד modifies the entire compound statement, not just the first or last element. Placing "exceedingly" at the beginning (LOGOS) makes it modify primarily "wicked" and weakens its application to the full compound. Placing it at the end (EMET) makes it fall on the whole: they were *wicked and sinful against the LORD* — and that wickedness and sinfulness was *exceedingly* so. The end-placement is structurally and semantically more faithful.

PNEUMA's "deeply wicked — flagrant sinners against the LORD" substitutes interpretive paraphrase for translation. "Flagrant" is a strong word that imports a connotation of brazenness and public exhibition that is not in the Hebrew; the Hebrew simply piles up "evil + sinful" with an intensifier. The council preserves the two Hebrew adjectives in their sequence.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 7 — Verse 14: Opening of the Divine Speech — "And the LORD said" vs. "The LORD said"

**Hebrew:** וַיהוָה אָמַר אֶל-אַבְרָם (*vaYHWH amar el-Avram*, "and the LORD said to Abram")
**Nature:** Register and discourse — how to render the waw at the opening of the divine speech
**Severity:** Low — affects the transition from the Sodom aside (v. 13) to the divine address

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "And the LORD said to Abram" | Waw rendered as "And"; maintains narrative flow; the temporal notice "after Lot had parted" follows as a subordinate clause |
| LOGOS | "The LORD said to Abram, after Lot had separated from him" | Drops the waw-consecutive conjunction entirely; treats the verse as a new narrative unit with its own independent opening |
| PNEUMA | "After Lot had parted from him, the LORD said to Abram" | Moves the temporal clause to the front for dramatic effect: the parting is acknowledged, then the divine speech follows |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "The LORD said to Abram, after Lot had separated from him" — LOGOS is followed with the temporal clause retained in its Hebrew position (after the main clause, not fronted).

EMET's "And the LORD said" is the literal rendering and is defensible, but "And" here risks reading as a mere additive conjunction when the verse has a specific structural relationship to what precedes it (the contrast between v. 13 — the wickedness of Sodom — and v. 14 — the LORD speaking to Abram). The contrast is better signaled by a fresh opening without "And."

PNEUMA's fronting of the temporal clause ("After Lot had parted... the LORD said") is a legitimate choice that heightens the dramatic effect: the departure is named first, then the divine word breaks the silence. The council appreciates the dramatic instinct but notes that the Hebrew places the main verb first (וַיהוָה אָמַר) and the temporal clause second (אַחֲרֵי הִפָּרֶד-לוֹט מֵעִמּוֹ). Maintaining this order preserves the Hebrew's emphasis: the LORD's speech is the primary fact; the timing of it (after Lot's departure) is the secondary, qualificatory element. LOGOS's structure achieves this most cleanly.

---

### DISAGREEMENT 8 — Verse 17: הִתְהַלֵּךְ — "Walk About" vs. "Walk Through" vs. "Walk"

**Hebrew:** קוּם הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ (*qum hithallek ba'arets le'arkhah ulerekhbah*)
**Nature:** Lexical-grammatical — the rendering of the Hithpael הִתְהַלֵּךְ and the spatial structure of the command
**Severity:** Moderate — the Hithpael's sustained traversal quality is theologically significant; the spatial dimensions (length and breadth) should be preserved

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "walk about in the land through its length and through its breadth" | "Walk about" for the Hithpael (sustained, back-and-forth traversal); "in the land" before "through its length" — closer to the Hebrew prepositional sequence |
| LOGOS | "walk through the length and the breadth of the land" | "Walk through" for the Hithpael; inverts the Hebrew phrase structure (the two dimensions lead; "the land" comes at the end) |
| PNEUMA | "Walk the length and breadth of the land" | Most compressed; drops the Hithpael's iterative quality entirely; "Walk" without any prepositional modifier is flatter than the Hebrew |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "walk through the land, through its length and through its breadth" — a synthesis between EMET and LOGOS.

The Hithpael הִתְהַלֵּךְ is grammatically important. EMET correctly identifies it as expressing sustained, repeated, back-and-forth traversal — not a single pass through but an ongoing, comprehensive movement. The verb is used of Enoch walking *with* God (5:22, 24) and of God himself walking *in* the garden (3:8). Its use here carries the sense of intimate, complete movement through a space. PNEUMA's simple "Walk" loses this texture.

"Walk about" (EMET) captures the iterative quality but reads in English as leisurely or aimless — "walking about" suggests wandering, not the purposeful comprehensive traversal the command envisions. "Walk through" (LOGOS) is more directional and purposeful.

The spatial phrase: Hebrew has בָּאָרֶץ לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ — "in the land, through its length and through its breadth." The two dimensions (length and breadth) are the two horizontal axes of a territory; together they constitute its full extent. LOGOS inverts the structure by placing the dimensions before the noun ("the length and breadth *of the land*"). The council prefers to preserve the Hebrew order: the land is named first (בָּאָרֶץ, "in the land"), then its dimensions follow ("through its length and through its breadth"), because this sequence enacts the command progressively: *enter the land* (בָּאָרֶץ), then *traverse its full extent* (לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ).

The adopted rendering "walk through the land, through its length and through its breadth" takes "walk through" from LOGOS (for purposeful traversal), places "the land" first (from the Hebrew and EMET's prepositional order), and then repeats "through" for the two dimensions (preserving the Hebrew's repetition of ל- in לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ).

---

### DISAGREEMENT 9 — Verse 18: Opening Construction and Verb Choices

**Hebrew:** וַיֶּאֱהַל אַבְרָם וַיָּבֹא וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא (*vayyehal Avram vayyavo vayyeshev be'eloney Mamre*)
**Nature:** Syntactic and lexical — the rendering of three consecutive wayyiqtol verbs and the handling of "pitched his tent"
**Severity:** Low-moderate — the three consecutive verbs give the verse a stately, unhurried cadence that is difficult to preserve in English

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|------------|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "And Abram pitched his tent and came and dwelt by the oaks of Mamre" | "Pitched his tent" for וַיֶּאֱהַל (the denominative verb from אֹהֶל, "tent"); "dwelt" for וַיֵּשֶׁב; preserves all three verbs; "And" opens the verse |
| LOGOS | "So Abram moved his tent and came and settled by the oaks of Mamre" | "Moved his tent" for וַיֶּאֱהַל; "settled" for וַיֵּשֶׁב; "So" opens the verse as a consequential conclusion |
| PNEUMA | "So Abram moved his tents and came to live near the great oaks of Mamre" | "Moved his tents" (plural — adding descriptive expansion); "came to live" collapses the two verbs וַיָּבֹא וַיֵּשֶׁב into one phrase; adds "great" for the oaks (not in MT) |

**SOPHIA's Ruling:** "So Abram moved his tent and came and settled by the oaks of Mamre" — LOGOS is followed, with minor adjustments.

**"So" vs. "And":** "So" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is the better opening particle here, for the same reason as verse 1: this verse is the consequential conclusion to the divine command of verse 17 ("arise, walk through the land, for I will give it to you"). Abram *does* arise and move: "So" marks that consequential movement. "And" (EMET) is too flat.

**"Moved his tent" vs. "pitched his tent":** The Hebrew verb וַיֶּאֱהַל (vayyehal) is a denominative verb from אֹהֶל (ohel, "tent") meaning "to tent, to pitch a tent, to encamp." Both "pitched his tent" (EMET) and "moved his tent" (LOGOS) are defensible. "Moved his tent" has been used at verse 12 for Lot's relocation; using "moved his tent" again at verse 18 for Abram's relocation creates a verbal parallel between the two tent-pitchings that the Hebrew sustains (both use the same root). The council follows LOGOS here.

**PNEUMA's additions are rejected:** "Moved his *tents*" (plural) — the MT has singular אֹהֶל; the plural is an addition without manuscript support. "Great oaks" — the word "great" is not in the MT; the Hebrew reads simply אֵלֹנֵי ("oaks of"). The addition, while evocative, is an interpretive expansion beyond the text.

**"Came and settled" vs. "came and dwelt":** The verb וַיֵּשֶׁב (vayyeshev) from יָשַׁב ("to sit, to dwell, to settle") — "settled" (LOGOS) carries a sense of permanent or intentional establishment, more than "dwelt" (EMET), which is neutral about duration and intention. Since this is Abram's final stopping point in the chapter's itinerary — his settlement at the location that will recur through the whole patriarchal cycle — "settled" is the more appropriate English word. The council adopts LOGOS.

---

## Agreements Across All Three Translators

The following were unanimous across EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA and required no council arbitration:

1. **"The LORD" for יְהוָה** — throughout the chapter.
2. **"The plain of the Jordan" for כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן** — all three agree; the LXX's τὸν περίχωρον ("the surrounding region") is rejected unanimously in favor of the MT's specific term.
3. **"Like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt" (v. 10)** — the double simile is preserved exactly by all three; the LXX's "paradise of God" is rejected unanimously in favor of MT's "garden of the LORD."
4. **"Dust of the earth" (v. 16)** — all three follow the MT over the LXX's "sand of the earth" unanimously and without hesitation; this is the chapter's most significant LXX variant and it is rejected by all three translators on the same grounds (the Adam/creation resonance of Hebrew עָפָר).
5. **"Your offspring" for זַרְעֲךָ** — consistent with the ruling at GEN_012 Disagreement 8; all three translators apply the established SOPHIA precedent.
6. **The proleptic parenthesis at verse 10 ("before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah")** — all three preserve it as a parenthetical narrative aside; none removes or softens it.
7. **"Against the LORD" for לַיהוָה (v. 13)** — all three follow the MT's adversarial preposition over the LXX's ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("before God").
8. **The four directional terms (v. 14): "northward and southward and eastward and westward"** — unanimous.
9. **"Oaks of Mamre" (v. 18) — plural** — all three follow the MT plural over the LXX singular.
10. **The MT throughout** — at every point of MT/LXX divergence, all three translators independently follow the MT. The council notes this unanimity; it is consistent with the SOPHIA pattern established in Genesis 1–12.

---

## Notes on EMET's Textual Contribution

EMET provided the most detailed engagement with the LXX variants for this chapter, producing the comparative table that documents ten MT/LXX divergences including the significant "dust" vs. "sand" substitution at verse 16 and the "garden" vs. "paradise" rendering at verse 10. EMET's analysis of the כָּבֵד wordplay between 12:10 and 13:2 is the most precise piece of lexical analysis in the chapter and is adopted in full in Council Note 1 of the consensus translation, even though EMET's preferred translation ("very heavy") is not adopted. EMET's extended notes on the מִקֶּדֶם directional theology (eastward movement as exile), the הִתְהַלֵּךְ Hithpael (walking as covenant possession), and the three-altar pattern (12:7, 12:8, 13:18) are all incorporated into the consensus notes. EMET's Dead Sea Scrolls and versional note (no significant DSS fragments for Genesis 13; SP agrees with MT throughout; Targum Onkelos renders the dust simile straightforwardly) is recorded for completeness.

---

## Notes on PNEUMA's Literary Contribution

PNEUMA provided the most sustained literary and theological analysis of the chapter, particularly on the Eden/Egypt/Sodom triangle in verse 10, the nature of Lot's sight-based choice as an echo of Eve's choice in Genesis 3:6, and the concept of "living by the word that stands outside the visible moment." PNEUMA's reading of Genesis 13 as a study in two modes of decision-making — sight versus promise — is the most fully articulated statement of the chapter's central meaning and is adopted as the structural framework for the consensus's opening analysis. PNEUMA's "penitential geography" observation about Abram's return to the altar (verses 3–4) is adopted into Council Note 2. PNEUMA's observation that the Jordan plain "looks like both [Eden and Egypt] simultaneously" as a "profound observation about the nature of visible beauty" is preserved in Council Note 5.

The council notes one PNEUMA addition that was rejected: the word "great" added to "the great oaks of Mamre" (v. 18) is not in the MT and was not adopted. The oaks are significant by the narrator's association of the site with Mamre and Hebron; no adjectival expansion is needed.

---

## Notes on LOGOS's Structural Contribution

LOGOS provided the clearest structural mapping of the chapter's three movements (return/conflict/separation/promise), the most precise formal analysis of the verse-by-verse syntactic decisions, and the most useful LXX comparison notes. LOGOS's identification of the "lifting up of eyes" parallel between verse 10 (Lot's self-directed gaze) and verse 14 (God's commanded gaze for Abram) is the most precise formal statement of the chapter's central contrast and is adopted in full in Council Notes 5 and 8. LOGOS's rendering of the conditional structure in verse 9 (explicit subject "you" with "left" and "right") informed the council's synthesis rendering for that verse.
